evals.prompt: We want to publish 5 blog posts per week, 3 newsletters per week, and 20 social posts per week. Our team is 2 marketers. Sustainable?
evals.expected_output: No — that's burnout cadence. Walks the sustainable-cadence framework. Recommends reducing to 1 blog/week, 1 newsletter/week, 3-5 social posts/week per primary platform, 1 video/week. Notes that consistency over 12 months beats volume over 6 weeks. References the team-capacity diagnostic questions.
assertions: Pushes back on the proposed cadence as unsustainable
Notes consistency over 12 months beats volume over 6 weeks
Output names a single highest-leverage next move

evals.prompt: Help me build a pillar/cluster model for my daily greens powder brand.
evals.expected_output: Walks the pillar/cluster framework with a worked example. Names 5-10 pillars (daily greens, afternoon energy, supplement stacking, sleep, etc.). For each pillar, names 5-10 clusters. Names the internal-linking rule (clusters link to pillar and 3-5 siblings). References ai-seo for AI-surface friendliness.
assertions: Provides a worked example with 5+ pillars

evals.prompt: We just published a 2,500-word pillar post about afternoon energy. Now what?
evals.expected_output: Walks the repurposing flow framework. Recommends 10-15 derivatives across newsletter, social, video, and ad creative substrate. Notes the discipline of planning derivatives before publishing (not after). Provides specific derivative plan: quote graphics, carousel, short-form videos, newsletter section, flow inserts. References social-strategy for platform-native repurposing and email-marketing for newsletter integration.
assertions: Notes derivatives should be planned before publishing
Lists specific derivative types (quotes, carousel, video, flow inserts)

evals.prompt: Our blog has 200 posts from the past 5 years. Should I delete the bad ones?
evals.expected_output: Yes — kill-or-update audit. Walks the kill-or-update framework. Provides criteria: rank check, email engagement, internal-link references, on-strategy-now check. Recommends 301-redirect or 410 for kills, not just unpublish. References seo-audit for the cannibalization and thin-content checks.
assertions: Names the four criteria (rank, engagement, links, on-strategy)

evals.prompt: Should we focus on evergreen content or reactive (newsy) content?
evals.expected_output: Walks the evergreen vs reactive framework. Recommends 60/40 for typical brands, 70/30 for SEO-heavy, 50/50 for social-heavy. Notes evergreen compounds; reactive produces peak reach but decays. Asks about channel mix to recommend the split. Notes the rule that reactive shouldn't contradict evergreen pillars.
assertions: Walks the evergreen vs reactive framework
Notes evergreen compounds and reactive decays
Notes reactive should reinforce evergreen, not contradict

evals.prompt: We publish content but it doesn't go anywhere. Distribution is an afterthought. Help.
evals.expected_output: Walks the distribution-built-in framework. Recommends every published piece have a planned distribution path before publish (newsletter slot, social derivatives, flow integration, internal links, ad creative substrate). Notes a piece without distribution gets the organic traffic it deserves and nothing else.
assertions: Lists the distribution channels per published piece
Notes pieces without distribution get only organic traffic

evals.prompt: What's the right content strategy for a brand that wants to win on AI search?
evals.expected_output: References ai-seo for the AI-surface specifics. Walks pillar/cluster as the AI-friendly structure. Recommends conversational-query optimization (pages answering questions in question form). Recommends FAQ sections per pillar. Notes that AI surfaces consume topical authority signals from internal linking and entity consistency.
assertions: Notes topical authority comes from linking and entity consistency
